org.name: Lances-Team
org.rationale: Knox is the secure knowledge repository for Lances-Team. It stores procedures, references, development logs and team knowledge across Live and Development projects. Live projects contain stable, production-ready documentation. Development projects hold work in progress, build histories and active logs.

projects.name: Knox — System Development
projects.description: Complete build history of the Knox knowledge repository system. Documents all architectural decisions, setup procedures, problems encountered and solutions found during initial development.

manuals.title: System Architecture
manuals.description: Overview of how Knox is structured — what each component does and how they connect.
manuals.author: Lance

sections.title: What Knox Is
sections.content:
Knox is a secure knowledge repository system built for Lances-Team. It stores procedures, references, development logs and team knowledge in organised projects.

Knox has two project tracks:
- LIVE PROJECTS: stable, production-ready documentation
- DEVELOPMENT PROJECTS: work in progress, drafts, logs

All data is stored as plain JSON and markdown on GitHub, making it AI-agnostic and portable.

sections.title: Component Map
sections.content:
SIGN-IN PAGE
  URL: https://lance-knox.netlify.app
  Host: Netlify (free tier, private GitHub repo compatible)
  File: index.html in repo root
  Purpose: Authentication gateway — access codes and biometric registration
  Storage: localStorage per browser (user accounts, invitation tokens)

REPOSITORY DATA
  File: repository.json (repo root)
  Contains: all projects, manuals, users, invitations
  Format: structured JSON, schema version knox-v3

CLAUDE SESSION
  File: CLAUDE_SESSION.md (repo root)
  Purpose: Loaded into Claude at session start — tells Claude what to fetch
  Fetches: repository.json, dev-log.md, notes.md, backups/manifest.json

CHATGPT SESSION
  File: CHATGPT_SESSION.md (repo root)
  Purpose: Equivalent session file for ChatGPT Plus/Pro with browsing
  Includes: explicit raw URLs, data structure explanation, working rules

BACKUP SYSTEM
  Workflow: .github/workflows/per-file-backups.yml
  Script: .github/scripts/backup.py
  Trigger: push to main affecting repository.json, active/**, logs/**
  Method: GitHub REST API (no git push — eliminates race conditions)
  Retention: 30 versions per file in backups/repository/, backups/notes/, backups/dev-log/, backups/active/
  Manifest: backups/manifest.json (auto-updated per run)

GITHUB REPO
  URL: https://github.com/lance505504-tech/Knox
  Visibility: Private
  Branch: main

sections.title: Folder Structure
sections.content:
Knox/
├── index.html                          Sign-in page (Netlify)
├── repository.json                     Live app state
├── CLAUDE_SESSION.md                   Claude session file
├── CHATGPT_SESSION.md                  ChatGPT session file
├── BACKUP_PROTOCOL.md                  Backup access and restore guide
├── RATIONALE.md                        System rationale and purpose
├── README.md                           Repo overview
├── active/
│   └── notes.md                        Live project notes
├── logs/
│   └── dev-log.md                      Development history
├── backups/
│   ├── manifest.json                   Auto-updated version index
│   ├── repository/                     30 versions of repository.json
│   ├── notes/                          30 versions of notes.md
│   ├── dev-log/                        30 versions of dev-log.md
│   └── active/                         30 versions of other active files
└── .github/
    ├── workflows/
    │   └── per-file-backups.yml        Backup automation
    └── scripts/
        └── backup.py                   Backup logic (GitHub API)

manuals.title: GitHub Repository Setup
manuals.description: How the Knox GitHub repository was created and configured, including the setup script and initial file structure.
manuals.author: Lance

sections.title: Setup Script
sections.content:
A Python script (knox-setup.py) was written to automate the entire repository creation.

The script:
- Prompts for GitHub token, username, repo name, org name
- Creates the private GitHub repository via API
- Creates all files and folders via GitHub Contents API
- Enables GitHub Pages (attempted — blocked on private repos on free plan)
- Prints the live URLs on completion

Key fix required: getpass.getpass() does not accept paste input in Windows PowerShell. Replaced with input() to allow token pasting.

Key fix required: Second run failed with 'sha not supplied' because files already existed. Fixed by fetching existing file SHA before PUT request.

All complex file content (workflow YAML, markdown files) was embedded in the script using json.dumps() to avoid Python string escaping issues.

sections.title: Running the Setup
sections.content:
Prerequisites:
1. GitHub account at github.com
2. Python installed (python.org — accept long path support prompt during install, requires reboot)
3. Personal Access Token (classic) with repo + workflow scopes
   Path: GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic)

Steps:
1. Download knox-setup.py to C:\cape31\Development\
2. Open PowerShell, navigate: cd C:\cape31\Development
3. Run: python knox-setup.py
4. Enter token (visible — input() not getpass)
5. Enter GitHub username: lance505504-tech
6. Enter repo name: Knox
7. Enter org name: Lances-Team
8. Script creates all files, confirms each one

Note: GitHub Pages requires paid plan for private repos. Switched to Netlify for hosting.

sections.title: Token Security Note
sections.content:
During setup, a GitHub Personal Access Token was inadvertently pasted into the Claude conversation and became visible in the chat history.

Action taken: Token was immediately revoked on GitHub and a new one generated.

Lesson: Always use getpass or similar for sensitive input, even in development. When tokens are exposed in any chat or log, revoke immediately.

Current token: New token generated after the exposure — the exposed token (ghp_Bltl2...) is no longer valid.

manuals.title: Backup System Development
manuals.description: The full history of building the automated backup system — problems encountered and how they were solved.
manuals.author: Lance

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
Each tracked file gets its own independent 30-version backup history:
  backups/repository/  — 30 versions of repository.json
  backups/notes/       — 30 versions of active/notes.md
  backups/dev-log/     — 30 versions of logs/dev-log.md
  backups/active/      — 30 versions of other active files

Files are named: YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS.ext
backups/manifest.json is auto-updated after each run listing all versions.

Trigger: push to main on paths: repository.json, active/**, logs/**
Backup commits use [skip ci] to prevent re-triggering.

sections.title: Problems and Fixes
sections.content:
PROBLEM 1: git push rejected — fast-forward error
CAUSE: Setup script committed 14 files rapidly, triggering 14 queued workflow runs competing to push simultaneously
FIX ATTEMPTS: git pull --rebase, git fetch + rebase, concurrency group — all failed due to race conditions
FINAL FIX: Rewrote workflow to use GitHub REST API instead of git push. Each backup file is created via API call — atomic, no push conflicts possible.

PROBLEM 2: Doom loop — workflow triggering itself
CAUSE: Backup commits were triggering new workflow runs
FIX: Added [skip ci] to all backup commit messages. Changed concurrency to cancel-in-progress: true.

PROBLEM 3: Python heredoc in YAML — silent failure
CAUSE: Python code inside YAML run: | block had less indentation than the block, causing YAML to terminate the block early. The Python script never actually ran.
FIX: Moved Python to a separate file (.github/scripts/backup.py). Workflow simply calls: python3 .github/scripts/backup.py

PROBLEM 4: Queued runs using old workflow version
CAUSE: GitHub uses the workflow file from the commit that triggered each run, not the current version. The 14 setup runs were all using old workflow versions.
FIX: Cancelled all pending runs manually via GitHub Actions UI, then committed the new workflow.

FINAL WORKING APPROACH:
- Workflow YAML: simple trigger + call python script
- Python script: uses GitHub REST API for all file operations
- No git commands, no git push, no race conditions
- concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true to handle bursts

sections.title: Current Workflow Files
sections.content:
Two files required:

1. .github/workflows/per-file-backups.yml
   - Trigger: push to main on tracked paths
   - Concurrency: knox-backup group, cancel-in-progress: true
   - Single step: python3 .github/scripts/backup.py
   - Env: GH_TOKEN and REPO passed from GitHub secrets

2. .github/scripts/backup.py
   - Gets changed files via git diff HEAD~1 HEAD
   - Creates timestamped backup of each changed file via GitHub API
   - Prunes each backup folder to 30 files max (deletes oldest)
   - Updates backups/manifest.json via API
   - All operations use [skip ci] commit messages
   - No git push used anywhere

manuals.title: Authentication and Sign-in Page
manuals.description: How the Knox sign-in page works, how user accounts and invitations are managed, and the biometric setup.
manuals.author: Lance

sections.title: How Authentication Works
sections.content:
The Knox sign-in page (index.html) handles authentication client-side using browser localStorage. No server required.

User accounts stored in: localStorage key 'ku'
Invitation tokens stored in: localStorage key 'ki'
Current session stored in: sessionStorage key 'kcu'

Access codes are SHA-256 hashed before storage — never stored in plain text.

Biometric credentials use WebAuthn (Passkeys) — the private key never leaves the device's secure enclave. Works with Face ID, Touch ID, and Android fingerprint on a real domain (not in iframes).

User roles: owner, admin, contributor, reader
Role hierarchy: owner > admin > contributor > reader
Owner can create any role. Admin can create contributors only.

sections.title: Creating Invitations
Hosting — Netlify
sections.content:
GitHub Pages was attempted first but requires a paid plan for private repositories.

Switched to Netlify (free tier):
- Connects directly to GitHub repo
- Deploys automatically on every push to main
- Serves index.html from repo root
- URL: https://lance-knox.netlify.app
- Custom name set in Netlify site configuration

Setup steps:
1. netlify.com → Sign up with GitHub
2. Add new site → Import existing project → GitHub → select Knox repo
3. Leave build settings blank (no build command, no publish directory — serves from root)
4. Deploy
5. Site configuration → Change site name → lance-knox

Biometric (WebAuthn) works on the Netlify domain because it is a real HTTPS domain. Does not work in Claude artifact iframes.

sections.title: Onboarding Materials Sent
sections.content:
2026-04-04 — Initial team setup

Sent to each team member:
- Individual invitation token (unique per person)
- Knox invitation email with rationale
- Knox-User-Guide.md — full user guide covering both Claude and ChatGPT sessions

User guide covers:
- Getting access and redeeming invitation
- Signing in (biometric and code)
- Which AI to use (Claude vs ChatGPT comparison table)
- Starting a session with Claude (CLAUDE_SESSION.md)
- Starting a session with ChatGPT (CHATGPT_SESSION.md)
- Creating, editing and searching manuals
- Project types (Live vs Development)
- Saving work back to GitHub
- Backup and version history
- Getting help

sections.title: Adding Future Team Members
sections.content:
1. Sign in to https://lance-knox.netlify.app
2. Open browser console (F12)
3. Run invitation script (see Authentication manual for full script)
4. Copy token from console
5. Send invitation email with:
   - Their personal token
   - Knox-User-Guide.md attached
   - URL: https://lance-knox.netlify.app
6. Update the Team section of this manual
7. Update CLAUDE_SESSION.md and CHATGPT_SESSION.md team rosters
8. Commit updated repository.json to GitHub

manuals.title: Session Files Reference
manuals.description: How the Claude and ChatGPT session files work and how to keep them updated.
manuals.author: Lance

sections.title: CLAUDE_SESSION.md
sections.content:
Location: repo root
Purpose: Loaded into Claude at the start of every session

Contains:
- Repo URL and raw base URL
- File registry with auto-load flags
- Team roster with roles
- Backup access instructions (tells Claude how to list and restore backups)
- Working rules (fetch from GitHub, never ask for local uploads)
- Project context (description of what Knox is used for)

How to use:
1. Download CLAUDE_SESSION.md from GitHub
2. Attach to a new Claude conversation
3. Type: load session
4. Claude fetches all auto-load files and confirms

Keep updated when:
- New team members are added
- New projects are created
- The repo URL changes

sections.title: CHATGPT_SESSION.md
sections.content:
Location: repo root
Purpose: Equivalent session file for ChatGPT Plus/Pro users

Differences from CLAUDE_SESSION.md:
- Includes exact full raw GitHub URLs (ChatGPT needs explicit URLs)
- Includes full explanation of repository.json data structure
- More explicit instructions since ChatGPT needs more guidance
- Notes that ChatGPT browsing must be enabled (globe icon)

How to use:
1. Download CHATGPT_SESSION.md from GitHub
2. Enable browsing in ChatGPT (globe icon or model settings)
3. Attach file to new conversation
4. Type: load session
5. ChatGPT browses to each URL and loads the data

Note: If ChatGPT says it cannot browse, start a new conversation and try again. Browsing is sometimes disabled by default.

sections.title: Using Knox with Other AIs
sections.content:
Knox is AI-agnostic. All data is plain JSON and markdown on GitHub — any AI with file upload and web access can work with it.

Knox has been designed and tested with:
- Claude (Anthropic) — primary AI, full session file
- ChatGPT (OpenAI) — full session file, browsing required

To adapt for other AIs (Gemini, Copilot, etc.):
- Copy CHATGPT_SESSION.md as a starting point
- Adjust instructions for how that AI handles file uploads and web browsing
- The GitHub raw URLs and data structure remain the same
- Commit the new session file to the repo root

The only Claude-specific component is the dark widget interface built in Claude artifacts. The underlying data and GitHub infrastructure works with any AI.

_meta.note: Initial repository.json created from Knox development session 2026-04-04
